data.title: Simplify-first: scale inference and complexity gates for brainstorm and implementation-plan
data.description:
## Context
Development teams using DSO encounter implementation proposals more complex than their actual scope warrants — unnecessary library dependencies, multi-layer fallback chains, and single-use abstractions — because brainstorm and implementation-plan skills do not anchor complexity decisions to confirmed scale evidence or present requirements. Agents optimize for hypothetical high-volume or future-reuse scenarios without verifying whether those conditions apply: a library added to optimize for volume that did not exist, a path resolution fallback chain when a relative path sufficed, and a shared abstraction for logic used only once. This creates review burden, maintenance overhead, and hard-to-reverse decisions.

## Success Criteria
- Brainstorm Phase 1 context loading includes a structured scale-evidence lookup — check design-notes.md and project configs/constants first, then perform a domain web search for volume estimates, then ask the user only if neither yields a usable estimate (defined as: a numeric order-of-magnitude value, or an explicit statement that volume is negligible or unbounded) — this lookup runs whenever the feature description implies a volume-sensitive decision (e.g., processing records, serving traffic, querying a data store)
- Brainstorm Phase 2 approach proposals always include at least one simple baseline option; any approach that adds a library dependency, performance optimization, or abstraction referenced by fewer than three existing call sites (Rule of Three) or that introduces a new interface, base class, or protocol type must cite the evidence that warrants it (scale evidence from the lookup, a confirmed user requirement, a correctness/security rationale, or an explicit user statement)
- scale-inference.md exists in plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/, defines the artifacts to domain web search to ask protocol, and is read by brainstorm Phase 1; if the file cannot be read at runtime, brainstorm emits an explicit error naming the missing file rather than silently proceeding
- complexity-gate.md exists in plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/, defines the six complexity gates (YAGNI, profiling-first, Rule of Three, dependency cost/benefit, scale threshold, LLM self-audit), and is read by both brainstorm Phase 2 and implementation-plan task proposal phase; if the file cannot be read, the calling skill emits an explicit error naming the missing file
- The approach-decision-maker agent Dimension 4 (Simplicity) rationale explicitly names which complexity-gate rule applied to each flagged proposal — when a proposal is flagged as over-engineered, the rationale includes the specific gate violated; when no gate fires, the rationale notes that the proposal satisfies all complexity gates
- A dogfooding validation confirms behavioral change: running brainstorm on a volume-sensitive feature description confirms (a) Phase 1 performs the scale-evidence lookup step, (b) Phase 2 includes a simple baseline option, and (c) any approach proposing a library dependency or performance optimization includes an evidence citation — verified against a checklist before epic closure

## Dependencies
None. This epic modifies planning-phase proposal generation and is orthogonal to any build-config epic that selects runtime commands from config — no shared gating logic or shared prompt files between the two.

## Approach
Create two shared prompt files (plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/scale-inference.md and plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/complexity-gate.md). Update brainstorm SKILL.md Phase 1 to read and apply scale-inference.md when the feature description implies volume-sensitive decisions, and Phase 2 to require complexity-gate.md compliance when proposing approaches. Update implementation-plan SKILL.md task proposal phase to read and apply complexity-gate.md before finalizing task implementations. Update the approach-decision-maker agent Dimension 4 evaluation criteria and anti-pattern detection to apply complexity-gate.md gates — additive only, no output contract changes.

Key implementation guidance from prompt alignment research: use prohibition framing with hard stops ("you may not proceed until X is stated") not preference language; require GATE/CHECKED/FINDING/VERDICT structured output for any complexity rationale; default scale estimate to "small scale unless evidence found" with explicit prohibition on upward interpolation; at implementation-plan task granularity, reduce gate check to YAGNI and Rule of Three only (the two gates checkable without external evidence).

## Scenario Analysis
- Complexity gate over-triggers on well-justified proposals (medium, runtime): gate criteria must include a justified-complexity path so evidence-backed complex options are not penalized
- Degenerate baselines for inherently complex domains (high, runtime): simple baseline requirement must clarify that the baseline is the simplest safe option, not a technically invalid stub
- Conflicting evidence citations across sessions (medium, runtime): scale-inference.md and complexity-gate.md are read independently per-session; if design-notes.md changes between brainstorm and implementation-plan runs, the two skills may reach different conclusions
- Silent skill degradation on missing shared prompt files (high, deployment): addressed by explicit error requirement in scale-inference.md and complexity-gate.md success criteria
- No configuration gate for enforcement level (medium, configuration): teams in regulated domains where over-engineering is a compliance requirement cannot disable the gate without modifying shared files directly
- Conflict with behavioral-testing-standard.md anti-patterns (medium, configuration): complexity-gate.md anti-pattern language must not overlap with behavioral-testing-standard.md rules to avoid conflicting instructions for test infrastructure proposals

## Prompt Alignment Findings
- Matched signal: skill file modifications and prompt templates
- Prior-art patterns: Production systems (Claude Code, OpenHands, fainir agent prompt) consistently use prohibition framing over preference framing. Modular delivery (one rule per named fragment) outperforms long rule lists. "Only when" gates outperform "prefer" language.
- Behavioral review: Three high-impact implementation requirements — (1) scale inference default must be "small scale unless evidence found" with explicit prohibition on upward interpolation; (2) simple baseline must be described in good faith, sandbagging prohibited unless grounded in Phase 1 evidence; (3) complexity-gate.md gates should use sequential hard stops not a flat checklist, and require structured GATE/CHECKED/FINDING/VERDICT fields in complexity rationale output.

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: user-request trigger
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 6
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none
- Follow-on scrutiny (Step 0): not triggered
- Feasibility resolution (Step 2.5): not triggered
- LLM-instruction signal (Step 5): triggered — keyword: skill file modifications
